revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八朔]]
{{Otheruses|旧暦の日付|その他|八朔 (曖昧さ回避)}}
'''Hassaku''' (八朔) refers to the first day of the eighth month (八月朔日) in the {{ill|lunisolar calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/lunisolar calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}}, corresponding to {{ill|August 1 (lunisolar calendar)|ja|8月1日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 1 (lunisolar calendar)|1=August 1 (Lunisolar)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q840103}}.
In the Gregorian calendar, this date shifts between approximately {{ill|August 25|en|August 25|qid=Q2819|lt=August 25|1=August 25}} and {{ill|September 23|en|September 23|qid=Q2888|lt=September 23|1=September 23}}, as the autumnal equinox falls within the eighth lunar month. Thus, the date is determined as either 29 days before or on the autumnal equinox itself.
Around this time, {{ill|Oryza sativa|lt=early rice|ja|早稲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oryza sativa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to イネ|1=Oryza sativa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q161426}} ripens, and a longstanding tradition among {{ill|Peasant|en|Peasant|qid=Q838811|lt=farmers|1=Peasant}} involved offering {{ill|first rice|ja|初穂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/first rice|qq=}} to benefactors. This custom is known as the '''Tanomi no Sekku''' (田の実節句). The word "tanomi" (田の実) also signifies "reliance" (頼み), leading to a practice among {{ill|samurai|ja|武家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Buke|qid=Q2927809}} and {{ill|Kuge|en|Kuge|qid=Q1196276|lt=Kuge|1=Kuge}} of exchanging gifts as a gesture of gratitude for mutual reliance.<ref>{{Cite book|title=Yearly Events Encyclopedia|author={{ill|Masayoshi Nishikado|ja|西角井正慶|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masayoshi Nishikado|1=Nishitsunoi Masayoshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11629502}}|year=1958|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|page=638}}</ref>
== History == The custom was already adopted as an official event by the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}}.<ref>Kenichi Futaki, "Hassaku of the Muromachi Shogunate," in ''Studies on Medieval Samurai Ceremonies'' (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985).</ref> The {{ill|Kamakura-fu|en|Kamakura-fu|qid=Q842731|lt=Kamakura-fu|1=Kamakura-fu}}, an administrative organ of the shogunate in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}, also conducted Hassaku ceremonies on August 1. Records in the {{ill|Kamakura Annual Events|ja|鎌倉年中行事|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamakura Annual Events|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} show that swords, {{ill|karamono|ja|唐物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/karamono|1=Karamono|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11418664}}, and horses were offered by local {{ill|Daimyo|en|Daimyo|qid=Q61982|lt=daimyō|1=Daimyo}} and temples to the {{ill|Kantō kubō|en|Kantō kubō|qid=Q860777|lt=Kamakura kubō|1=Kantō kubō}}, who reciprocated with gifts such as swords, karamono, and horses. This influence extended to records of the custom among the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|qid=Q431928|lt=Late Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} and {{ill|Utsunomiya clan|ja|宇都宮氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utsunomiya clan|1=Utsunomiya clan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11449780}} during the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117415390}}.<ref>Kuniaki Yamada, "Hassaku of the Kamakura-fu," in ''Kamakura-fu and Regional Society'' (Dōseisha, 2014).</ref>
{{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} is said to have officially entered {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|qid=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} for the first time on Hassaku, specifically on {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qid=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 18, August 1 (Gregorian date: {{ill|August 30, 1590|ja|1590th year 8月30日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/August 30, 1590|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Consequently, the {{ill|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|ja|Tokugawa shogunate|1=Tokugawa shogunate|2=ja|3=江戸幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} considered this day second only to New Year as a celebratory occasion.<ref>''Yearly Events Encyclopedia,'' p. 639.</ref> However, the diary of {{ill|Matsudaira Ietada (Fukōzu)|en|Matsudaira Ietada (Fukōzu)|qid=Q6788035|lt=Matsudaira Ietada|1=Matsudaira Ietada (Fukōzu)}} notes that the actual entry occurred on July 18. It is believed that the settlement of territories among Kantō daimyō, known as the {{ill|Kantō kokubun|ja|関東国分|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō kokubun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was completed on August 1 with the determination of {{ill|Satake Yoshinobu|en|Satake Yoshinobu|qid=Q1071525|lt=Satake Yoshinobu's|1=Satake Yoshinobu}} domain, formalizing Tokugawa's new territories under the {{ill|Toyotomi regime|ja|豊臣政権|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyotomi regime|1=Toyotomi government|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11634225}}.<ref>Hiroyuki Shiba, ''Tokugawa Ieyasu: From Boundary Lord to Unifier of the Realm,'' Heibonsha, 2017. ISBN 978-4-582-47731-3, p. 193.</ref>
After the {{ill|Meiji calendar reform|ja|明治改暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji calendar reform|1=Meiji calendar reform|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q125315161}}, the event shifted to either {{ill|August 1|en|August 1|qid=Q2788|lt=August 1 (solar calendar)|1=August 1}} or {{ill|September 1 (delayed by a month)|ja|月遅れ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/September 1 (delayed by a month)|1=month delay|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11516230}}.
== Local Festivals == === Kumamoto's Hassaku Festival === [[File:熊本県上益城郡山都町.jpg|thumb|Large festival floats at the Hassaku Festival in Yamato Town, Kumamoto]] In {{ill|Yamato, Kumamoto|ja|熊本県上益城郡山都町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato, Kumamoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the {{ill|Hassaku|lt=Hassaku|lt=Hassaku Festival|ja|八朔祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hassaku Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八朔}} takes place annually on the first Saturday and Sunday of September, close to the average date of the old calendar's August 1. Renowned for its massive "crafted floats" (造り物), this festival dates back to the mid-{{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} and expresses gratitude to the {{ill|Ta-no-Kami|en|Ta-no-Kami|qid=Q7683716|lt=Ta-no-Kami|1=Ta-no-Kami}} while marking the harvest.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kumamoto Yamato Tourism Information|url=http://www.town.kumamoto-yamato.lg.jp/ka/syokokankoka/kanko/index.jsp}}</ref>
Large floats measuring 3–4 meters in height and 7–8 meters in length are paraded through the town. The event draws numerous visitors and photographers. Celebrations also include a nighttime fireworks display near the {{ill|Tsūjun Bridge|en|Tsūjun Bridge|qid=Q3077059|lt=Tsūjun Bridge|1=Tsūjun Bridge}}, a nationally designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|qid=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Property|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.
=== Fukui's Hassaku Festival === In {{ill|Mihama, Fukui|ja|福井県美浜町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mihama, Fukui|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the {{ill|Shinjō District|ja|新庄区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinjō District|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} celebrates Hassaku with rituals for abundant harvests and prosperity. A parade, accompanied by traditional music, carries a barrel shrine (樽神輿) to {{ill|Hiyoshi Shrine|ja|日吉神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiyoshi Shrine|1=Hiyoshi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11505065}}. A tengu figure carrying a phallic {{ill|Shintai|en|Shintai|qid=Q327532|lt=Shintai|1=Shintai}} playfully interacts with onlookers, with a belief that women touched by the object will be blessed with fertility.<ref>Fukui Shimbun, "Tengu Causes a Stir at the Hassaku Festival," September 1, 2012.</ref>
=== Other Regional Customs === In {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, geisha and maiko traditionally visit their tea houses and instructors' homes on the Gregorian calendar's August 1.
In {{ill|Ashiya, Fukuoka|ja|福岡県芦屋町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashiya, Fukuoka|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Hassaku is celebrated as the "Boy's Festival" (八朔の節句), featuring straw horses (わら馬) for boys and rice flour dolls (だごびーな) for girls. Recognized as a tradition over 300 years old, this custom is listed as a {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Intangible Folk Cultural Properties Requiring Documentation|lt=Record-worthy intangible folk cultural asset|ja|記録作成等の措置を講ずべき無形の民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/record-worthy intangible folk cultural asset|qq=}}.
== Related Food == The citrus fruit {{ill|Hassaku|en|Hassaku|qid=Q130172|lt=Hassaku|1=Hassaku}} derives its name from this festival, as it ripens around August 1.
== References == <references/>
== External Links == * [http://www.town.kumamoto-yamato.lg.jp/ka/syokokankoka/kanko/index.jsp Hassaku Festival (Yamato Town, Kumamoto)] * [http://marugame.town-web.net/tyuseisan/dangouma_1.htm Hassaku Dango Horses (Marugame City)]
{{translated page|ja|八朔|version=102687620|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:八朔]]
